words.shortDefinition: Lightweight, standalone, executable software package containing code and dependencies for isolation.
words.simpleDefinition: Portable software unit bundling application code with its runtime, system tools, and libraries — ensuring consistent execution across environments. It provides isolated process space while sharing the host kernel for efficiency.
words.longDefinition: Containers leverage Linux kernel features like namespaces and control groups to create isolated environments for processes. Unlike virtual machines, they do not include a guest OS, making them significantly faster to start and more resource-efficient. This isolation ensures that the application behaves identically whether running on a developer's laptop, a test server, or a production cluster, effectively solving the 'it works on my machine' problem.
words.connector: Think of a container like a standardized shipping box — it protects the contents and fits perfectly onto any compatible ship, train, or truck.
words.techContext: The fundamental unit of modern DevOps, allowing developers to say 'it works on my machine' and know it will work in production.
words.exampleSentence: We spun up a new container for the worker process to handle the sudden spike in background tasks.

codeExample.code:
# Run an interactive container
docker run -it --name my-app-instance node:20-alpine sh

words.shortDefinition: Immutable, read-only template containing instructions and snapshots for creating containers.
words.simpleDefinition: Read-only blueprint for a container composed of layered file systems — representing a specific point-in-time state of an application. Images are immutable and cached locally to speed up deployments and ensure version consistency.
words.longDefinition: A Docker image is built from a Dockerfile and consists of a series of read-only layers. Each layer represents an instruction in the image's history. When you start a container, a writable layer is added on top of these read-only layers. This design allows images to be highly portable and shared across different systems through registries, serving as the definitive source for application deployment.
words.connector: Think of a Docker image like a master blueprint — you use it to build as many identical houses as you need.
words.techContext: You can think of an image as a class and a container as an instance of that class.
words.exampleSentence: I pulled the official Postgres image from Docker Hub to use as the base for our database container.

codeExample.code:
# Build an image from a Dockerfile in the current directory
docker build -t my-app:v1.0 .

words.shortDefinition: Text-based script containing sequential commands to automate Docker image creation.
words.simpleDefinition: Automated build script defining the environment, dependencies, and configuration for an image — enabling reproducible infrastructure as code. It translates human-readable instructions into the binary layers that form a portable container image.
words.longDefinition: The Dockerfile serves as the primary mechanism for automating image construction. It starts with a base image and then executes a series of instructions such as RUN for commands, COPY for files, and ENV for variables. By version-controlling the Dockerfile alongside the source code, teams can guarantee that the build environment remains identical across the entire development lifecycle, facilitating reliable CI/CD pipelines and easier troubleshooting.
words.connector: Think of a Dockerfile like a recipe card — anyone with the same ingredients can follow the steps to produce the exact same dish.
words.techContext: The 'recipe' for your environment. It should be kept in version control alongside your code.
words.exampleSentence: I updated the Dockerfile to include the latest security patches for our Node.js runtime.

codeExample.code:
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install --production
COPY . .
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "index.js"]

words.shortDefinition: Networking configuration linking host ports to isolated container network ports.
words.simpleDefinition: Bridge between the host's public network and a container's private virtual network — exposing specific services to the outside world. It allows external traffic to reach applications running inside the container's isolated network environment.
words.longDefinition: Because containers run in an isolated network namespace, their internal ports are not automatically accessible from the host machine or external network. Port mapping creates a rule in the host's network stack that forwards traffic from a host-side port to a container-side port. This allows you to run multiple containers using the same internal port, such as 80, by mapping them to different unique ports on the host.
words.connector: Think of port mapping like a building's switchboard — calls to specific extensions are routed to the correct private office inside.
words.techContext: We mapped host port 80 to container port 3000 so that we could access our Node app via 'localhost' in the browser.
words.exampleSentence: I checked the port mapping because I couldn't connect to the database container on the default port.

codeExample.code:
# Map host port 8080 to container port 80
docker run -p 8080:80 nginx:alpine

words.shortDefinition: Foundational starting point for building custom Docker images.
words.simpleDefinition: The initial filesystem layer specified in a Dockerfile — providing the operating system and basic utilities for an application. It serves as the parent upon which all subsequent application-specific layers and configurations are stacked and saved.
words.longDefinition: Every Dockerfile begins with a FROM instruction that specifies the base image. This image provides the root filesystem and the core libraries required for the container to function. You might choose a broad base like Ubuntu for its extensive package support, or a minimal one like Alpine to keep the image small. The choice of base image directly impacts the security, size, and performance of the resulting containerized application.
words.connector: Think of a base image like a blank canvas — it’s the starting foundation that you add your own creative work onto.
words.techContext: Choosing a small, secure base image like 'alpine' is a best practice for container security and performance.
words.exampleSentence: We changed our base image to a minimal Debian-slim to fix some compatibility issues we had with Alpine's musl library.

codeExample.code:
# Start from a specific version of Node.js as the base
FROM node:20-slim

words.shortDefinition: Public cloud-based registry for sharing and discovering containerized software images.
words.simpleDefinition: The largest community-driven repository for Docker images — hosting official versions of databases, runtimes, and open-source tools. It serves as the default source for pulling images, enabling rapid development and standardized software distribution across the globe.
words.longDefinition: Docker Hub acts as the central nerve center for the Docker ecosystem, providing a platform where developers can publish and download container images. It includes features like automated builds, security scanning, and private repositories. Most developers start their projects by pulling an official image from Docker Hub, which has been vetted and optimized for containerized environments. This collaborative library significantly reduces the time required to set up complex development stacks.
words.connector: Think of Docker Hub like GitHub for container images — it’s the place where the world shares and versions their software.
words.techContext: I searched Docker Hub to find a pre-configured image for our CI testing environment.
words.exampleSentence: We use Docker Hub to pull our base images, but we host our proprietary code images in our own private registry.

codeExample.code:
# Search for official images on Docker Hub
docker search --filter "is-official=true" redis

words.shortDefinition: Stackable, intermediate file system changes created during the Docker build process.
words.simpleDefinition: Incremental filesystem snapshots created for each instruction in a Dockerfile — enabling efficient caching and storage. These read-only segments are stacked to form the final image, allowing Docker to reuse unchanged layers across builds.
words.longDefinition: Docker uses a union file system to stack layers, which are essentially diffs from the previous state. When a Dockerfile instruction is modified, Docker only rebuilds that layer and those following it, significantly reducing build times through caching. This architecture also saves disk space because multiple images can share the same underlying base layers, only storing the unique changes introduced in their respective build processes.
words.connector: Think of image layers like a stack of transparent slides — each slide adds more detail until the final image is complete.
words.techContext: Understanding layers is key to optimizing build times. Put instructions that change frequently (like COPY .) at the end of the Dockerfile.
words.exampleSentence: By reordering our Dockerfile, we ensured that the 'npm install' layer is cached unless the package.json file actually changes.

codeExample.code:
# View the layers that make up an image
docker history my-app:v1.0

words.etymology: from Middle English leyer ('that which lies').

words.shortDefinition: Persistent data storage mechanism decoupled from the container's ephemeral lifecycle.
words.simpleDefinition: Dedicated storage managed by Docker to persist data independently of container lifecycles — ensuring information survives restarts and deletions. Volumes bridge the gap between temporary container storage and permanent host or network filesystems.
words.longDefinition: Volumes are the preferred mechanism for persisting data generated by and used by Docker containers. While container filesystems are wiped when the container is removed, volumes are stored in a part of the host filesystem managed by Docker. They are easier to back up, more performant than bind mounts on certain platforms, and allow multiple containers to share the same data concurrently without corruption.
words.connector: Think of a volume like an external hard drive — you can plug it into different computers and your files remain safe.
words.techContext: Essential for databases. You mount a volume to /var/lib/postgresql/data so your data survives a container restart.
words.exampleSentence: We mapped a Docker volume to the uploads directory so that user-uploaded images wouldn't disappear when we redeployed the app.

codeExample.code:
# Create a volume and mount it to a container
docker volume create pgdata
docker run -d -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres

words.shortDefinition: Centralized service for storing, versioning, and distributing named Docker images.
words.simpleDefinition: Storage and content delivery system for managing Docker images — facilitating sharing across teams and deployment pipelines. Registries serve as the authoritative source for containers, allowing developers to push new versions and servers to pull them.
words.longDefinition: A Docker registry is a system that allows you to store and distribute images. It organizes images into repositories, which can contain multiple tagged versions. Public registries like Docker Hub host official software images, while private registries are used by organizations to secure their proprietary code. Integration with CI/CD systems allows for automated image publishing, ensuring that the latest validated build is always available for deployment.
words.connector: Think of a registry like an app store for containers — it’s where you publish your work so others can download it.
words.techContext: Our CI/CD pipeline automatically builds a new image and pushes it to our private Azure Container Registry after every successful merge.
words.exampleSentence: The deployment failed because the production server didn't have the correct credentials to pull the image from our private registry.

codeExample.code:
# Log in to a registry and push an image
docker login myregistry.com
docker push myregistry.com/my-app:v1.2

words.shortDefinition: Orchestration tool for defining and running multi-container applications via YAML.
words.simpleDefinition: YAML-driven tool for managing complex, multi-container environments as a single stack — automating network and volume configuration. It enables developers to launch entire application suites, including databases and caches, with a single standardized command.
words.longDefinition: Docker Compose is essential for managing applications that consist of multiple interdependent services. By defining the entire stack in a docker-compose.yml file, you can specify environment variables, port mappings, and service dependencies. This eliminates the need for complex, error-prone shell scripts to start your infrastructure. It is particularly powerful for local development and testing, ensuring that everyone on the team is running the exact same stack of services.
words.connector: Think of Docker Compose like a conductor's score — it tells every instrument what to play so they all sound good together.
words.techContext: The standard way to manage a local development environment consisting of a web app, a database, and a cache.
words.exampleSentence: I added a Redis service to our docker-compose.yml so we can test the new caching logic locally.

codeExample.code:
version: '3.8'
services:
  api:
    build: .
    ports: ["8080:8080"]
  db:
    image: postgres:15-alpine

words.shortDefinition: Security-focused, lightweight Linux distribution commonly used for minimal Docker images.
words.simpleDefinition: Minimalist Linux distribution built on musl libc and BusyBox — weighing only 5MB as a base image. It is favored by Docker users for its tiny footprint, rapid pull times, and reduced surface area for security vulnerabilities.
words.longDefinition: Alpine Linux is designed with security and resource efficiency in mind. Its small size makes it perfect for containers, where minimizing image volume is crucial for fast scaling and low storage costs. However, because it uses musl libc instead of the more common glibc found in Ubuntu or Debian, some binaries may require additional configuration or recompilation to run correctly, necessitating careful testing during the migration process.
words.connector: Think of Alpine Linux like a tiny studio apartment — it’s stripped of all non-essentials to maximize efficiency and save space.
words.techContext: Most of our microservices use the alpine tag to keep our container registry usage low and deployments fast.
words.exampleSentence: Using the Node-Alpine image helped us cut our cold-start times in half because the image layer downloads so quickly.

codeExample.code:
# Use Alpine for a tiny Python image
FROM python:3.11-alpine
RUN apk add --no-cache gcc musl-dev

words.etymology: Named after the Alps mountains, implying something small and clean.

words.shortDefinition: Semantic labeling system for identifying specific versions and states of images.
words.simpleDefinition: Alias-based versioning for Docker images — allowing developers to mark specific builds with identifiers like 'v1.0.1' or 'stable'. Effective tagging strategies are crucial for deployment workflows, ensuring that teams can precisely control which code version runs.
words.longDefinition: Image tags are human-readable aliases for the long SHA-256 hashes that uniquely identify image builds. While 'latest' is the default tag, it can be dangerous in production because it is mutable and changes over time. Best practices involve using semantic versioning or git commit hashes as tags, providing a clear audit trail and enabling reliable rollbacks to previous known-good states when a new deployment encounters issues in the field.
words.connector: Think of image tags like bookmarks in a long book — they help you quickly find and return to the exact page.
words.techContext: Never use the 'latest' tag in production; always use a specific version tag to ensure your environment is reproducible.
words.exampleSentence: I tagged the new build as 'v2.1.0-beta' so the QA team could pull and test it specifically.

codeExample.code:
# Tag a local image for a specific version
docker tag app-image:latest my-repo/app-image:v2.4.5

words.shortDefinition: Short-lived, temporary nature of a container's local file system.
words.simpleDefinition: The design principle where container instances are treated as temporary and disposable — where local data is lost upon deletion. This encourages a stateless architectural approach, where persistent information is strictly managed through external volumes or databases rather than local storage.
words.longDefinition: In the Docker ecosystem, 'ephemeral' refers to the fact that any changes made to the container's writable layer are non-persistent. When a container is stopped and removed, that layer is deleted forever. This philosophy forces developers to build more resilient and scalable applications by separating the application logic from the data it processes. It ensures that any container can be replaced or moved to a different host without losing critical application state.
words.connector: Think of ephemeral like a whiteboard — you can write on it during a meeting, but it’s erased once the meeting is over.
words.techContext: Containers should be treated as ephemeral; you should be able to kill and replace any container at any time without losing critical state.
words.exampleSentence: Since the local container storage is ephemeral, we store all application logs in an external logging service.

codeExample.code:
# Run a container that is automatically removed after it exits
docker run --rm alpine echo "I am temporary"

words.etymology: from Greek ephēmeros ('lasting only a day').

words.shortDefinition: Direct mapping of host machine directories into a container's filesystem.
words.simpleDefinition: High-performance storage method that maps a specific host path into a container — providing immediate synchronization between host and container files. They are indispensable for development, allowing developers to see code changes instantly without rebuilding the image.
words.longDefinition: Bind mounts have been available since the early days of Docker. When you use a bind mount, a file or directory on the host machine is mounted into a container. The file or directory is referenced by its absolute path on the host machine. Unlike volumes, bind mounts rely on the host machine having a specific directory structure. This makes them less portable than volumes but perfect for development-time source code sharing.
words.connector: Think of a bind mount like a shared folder between your laptop and a VM — changes on either side appear instantly.
words.techContext: I used a bind mount to map my local 'src' folder into the container so I could see my code changes immediately without rebuilding the image.
words.exampleSentence: Bind mounts are great for development, but for production data, Docker volumes are generally preferred for better performance and management.

codeExample.code:
# Mount current directory to /app inside the container
docker run -v $(pwd):/app -w /app node:20-alpine npm run dev

words.shortDefinition: Fixed primary command executed when a container launches.
words.simpleDefinition: Immutable startup instruction defining the main executable for a container — ensuring consistent behavior by ignoring typical override arguments. It is often paired with CMD to provide default parameters while keeping the core application command constant.
words.longDefinition: The ENTRYPOINT instruction allows you to configure a container that will run as an executable. When you use the 'exec' form, Docker runs the specified command as PID 1, allowing it to receive Unix signals like SIGTERM properly. Unlike CMD, the entrypoint is not overridden when command-line arguments are passed; instead, those arguments are appended to the entrypoint, making it ideal for wrapper scripts or dedicated service containers.
words.connector: Think of the entrypoint like the ignition in a car — it’s the one action that always starts the engine running.
words.techContext: Often used for initialization scripts that set up a database or environment before starting the main application process.
words.exampleSentence: We used an entrypoint script to run database migrations before starting the web server.

codeExample.code:
# Ensure the container always runs as an executable
ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/bin/python3", "main.py"]

words.shortDefinition: Default execution command and parameters for a running container instance.
words.simpleDefinition: Dockerfile instruction providing fallback commands and arguments — which are easily overridden by user input at runtime. It defines the default entry point for the container's main process while remaining flexible enough for various operational needs and debugging.
words.longDefinition: The CMD instruction sets the command to be executed when a container starts, but it is less permanent than ENTRYPOINT. If a user provides arguments to the 'docker run' command, the CMD values are completely ignored in favor of the new input. In a Dockerfile, CMD is often used to provide default arguments to an ENTRYPOINT, creating a flexible interface where the main application stays fixed but its behavior can be tweaked easily.
words.connector: Think of CMD like the default settings on a new device — they work out of the box but are easy to change.
words.techContext: If you provide an ENTRYPOINT, CMD becomes the default arguments for that entrypoint.
words.exampleSentence: I used CMD to set the default log level for our app, which developers can override if they need more verbose output.

codeExample.code:
# Set the default command, overrideable by 'docker run'
CMD ["npm", "run", "serve"]

words.shortDefinition: Background process managing Docker objects and receiving API requests from clients.
words.simpleDefinition: Persistent background service — dockerd — that handles the heavy lifting of building, running, and distributing containers. It exposes a REST API that the Docker CLI communicates with to manage the lifecycle of various Docker objects.
words.longDefinition: The Docker daemon is the heart of the Docker architecture. It runs on the host machine and listens for requests from the Docker client. It is responsible for managing images, containers, networks, and volumes. The daemon can also communicate with other daemons to manage Docker services in a cluster. Because it requires root privileges to manage system resources, security configuration of the daemon is critical in production environments.
words.connector: Think of the Docker daemon like an engine room on a ship — you give orders from the bridge, and it works.
words.techContext: When you run 'docker build', your CLI client is actually sending a request to the Docker daemon running on your machine.
words.exampleSentence: I had to restart the Docker daemon because it was having trouble communicating with the container runtime.

codeExample.code:
# Check the status of the Docker daemon (on Linux)
systemctl status docker

words.etymology: from Greek daimōn ('spirit, guardian'), used in computing for background processes.

words.shortDefinition: Dockerfile optimization technique using multiple build phases to reduce image size.
words.simpleDefinition: Advanced Dockerfile pattern using separate stages for building and running — discarding heavy build tools in the final image. It allows developers to ship only the compiled binary, drastically reducing the container's security attack surface and footprint.
words.longDefinition: Multi-stage builds solve the problem of images becoming bloated with build-time dependencies like compilers, header files, and package managers. By using multiple FROM instructions, you can perform intensive compilation in an initial stage and then copy only the resulting artifacts into a clean, minimal runtime image. This results in significantly smaller production images, faster deployment times, and improved security, as no unnecessary tools are left behind for potential attackers.
words.connector: Think of a multi-stage build like cooking a meal — you use many messy tools in the kitchen, but serve only the food.
words.techContext: We used a multi-stage build to compile our Go app in a 500MB image, then copied the binary into a 5MB alpine image for production.
words.exampleSentence: Switching to multi-stage builds reduced our final image size from 1.2GB down to just 80MB.

codeExample.code:
# Build stage
FROM golang:1.21-alpine AS build
COPY . .
RUN go build -o server

# Run stage
FROM alpine:latest
COPY --from=build /server /server
CMD ["/server"]

words.shortDefinition: Native clustering and orchestration tool integrated into the Docker engine.
words.simpleDefinition: Integrated orchestration platform for managing multiple Docker hosts as a single virtual cluster — providing built-in service scaling and discovery. It simplifies distributed deployments by allowing developers to define service states that Docker automatically maintains across nodes.
words.longDefinition: Docker Swarm mode is built directly into the Docker Engine, allowing you to turn a group of Docker hosts into a cluster. It uses a declarative model to define the desired state of services, such as how many replicas of a container should be running. Swarm handles load balancing, health checks, and rolling updates, making it an excellent, lightweight alternative to Kubernetes for teams that prioritize simplicity and Docker-native integration.
words.connector: Think of Docker Swarm like a manager for a group of employees — it assigns tasks and makes sure the work continues.
words.techContext: While Kubernetes is more powerful, Docker Swarm is often preferred for smaller projects because it is much simpler to set up and manage.
words.exampleSentence: We used Docker Swarm to deploy our application across three servers for high availability.

codeExample.code:
# Initialize a swarm cluster and deploy a service
docker swarm init
docker service create --replicas 3 --name web-service nginx

words.shortDefinition: Minimal images containing only an application and its runtime dependencies.
words.simpleDefinition: Hardened container images lacking shells, package managers, or standard Unix utilities — containing only the application binary. They provide the highest level of container security by strictly limiting the tools available to an attacker.
words.longDefinition: Distroless images take the concept of minimalism further than Alpine. They contain only your application and its dependencies, stripped of everything else you would expect in a Linux distribution. Since there is no shell like bash or package manager like apt, common exploit techniques that rely on local tools are effectively neutralized. This makes distroless images an industry best practice for production environments where security is the highest priority.
words.connector: Think of a distroless image like a high-security vault — it only contains the treasure, with no tools left inside for thieves.
words.techContext: For our most critical security services, we use Google's distroless images to ensure that even if an attacker gets in, they don't even have a shell to run commands.
words.exampleSentence: We moved our production API to a distroless image to eliminate vulnerabilities related to unused OS utilities like curl or bash.

codeExample.code:
# Build app in one stage, run in distroless
FROM golang:1.21 AS build
COPY . .
RUN go build -o app

FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12
COPY --from=build /app /app
CMD ["/app"]

words.shortDefinition: Linux kernel feature limiting and isolating resource usage for container processes.
words.simpleDefinition: Kernel mechanism — cgroups — used to monitor and restrict hardware resource consumption like CPU and memory. It prevents 'noisy neighbors' by ensuring that no single container can exhaust the host system's resources, maintaining overall stability.
words.longDefinition: Control groups, or cgroups, are a Linux kernel feature that allows Docker to partition resources such as CPU time, system memory, and network bandwidth. By assigning containers to specific cgroups, the Docker daemon can enforce limits defined at runtime. This isolation is critical for multi-tenant environments where you must guarantee that a runaway process in one container does not crash the entire host or starve other services of vital resources.
words.connector: Think of cgroups like a fuse box in a house — it prevents one faulty appliance from blowing the power for everyone.
words.techContext: When you run a container with '--memory=512m', Docker is actually using cgroups under the hood to enforce that limit.
words.exampleSentence: The container was killed by the OOM (Out Of Memory) killer because it exceeded the limit set by its control group.

codeExample.code:
# Run a container with limited CPU and memory resources
docker run -d --cpus="0.5" --memory="256m" nginx

words.shortDefinition: Linux kernel feature providing isolated views of system resources for containers.
words.simpleDefinition: Core isolation technology that provides containers with their own virtualized view of system resources — including process IDs and network stacks. It ensures processes in one container cannot see or interact with those in another or the host.
words.longDefinition: Namespaces are the primary building block of container isolation in Linux. They wrap a global system resource in an abstraction that makes it appear to the processes within the namespace that they have their own isolated instance of the resource. Different types of namespaces provide isolation for different aspects of the system, such as PIDs (process IDs), NET (networking), MNT (mount points), and UTS (hostname), effectively creating the illusion of a separate OS.
words.connector: Think of namespaces like private soundproof booths in a busy library — everyone is in one room, but can't hear each other.
words.techContext: Namespaces are the reason why 'process 1' inside a container is not the same as 'process 1' on the host machine.
words.exampleSentence: Docker uses network namespaces to give each container its own virtual network stack and IP address.

codeExample.code:
# See different PIDs inside and outside the container
docker run alpine ps aux

words.shortDefinition: Distributed virtual network enabling communication between containers on different host nodes.
words.simpleDefinition: Virtual network spanning multiple physical servers — allowing containers across a cluster to communicate securely as if on a local subnet. It abstracts the underlying physical network to provide seamless service discovery in distributed environments.
words.longDefinition: Overlay networks are used in the context of multi-host container clusters, such as Docker Swarm or Kubernetes. They use VXLAN technology to encapsulate traffic and route it between containers regardless of which physical host they reside on. This allows for high-level abstractions where services can be scaled across many nodes without the need to manage individual IP addresses or complex firewall rules for every cross-node communication path.
words.connector: Think of an overlay network like a private VPN for containers — it lets them talk privately across the entire internet.
words.techContext: Essential for Docker Swarm and Kubernetes, where containers are spread across a cluster of servers.
words.exampleSentence: The microservices communicate over an overlay network, so they don't need to know the physical IP addresses of the servers they are running on.

words.shortDefinition: Lightweight, industry-standard container runtime for managing complete container lifecycles.
words.simpleDefinition: High-level container runtime that handles image transfer, storage, and execution across host systems — serving as a core component of Docker. It is designed to be embedded into larger systems like Kubernetes, focusing strictly on performance and reliability.
words.longDefinition: containerd is an industry-standard container runtime that was originally part of Docker but is now an independent project under the Cloud Native Computing Foundation. It handles all the low-level tasks required to run containers, such as managing namespaces and cgroups via runc. By decoupling the runtime from the higher-level Docker features, containerd provides a stable and secure foundation that can be used by various orchestration platforms to execute containerized workloads efficiently.
words.connector: Think of containerd like the engine of a car — while the car has seats and a radio, the engine does the work.
words.techContext: Docker itself now uses containerd under the hood to handle the actual running of containers.
words.exampleSentence: Since Kubernetes deprecated Docker-shim, we now use containerd directly as our container runtime on all our nodes.

codeExample.code:
# View containerd namespaces (advanced troubleshooting)
ctr namespaces list

words.shortDefinition: Modern, high-performance storage driver using union filesystems for image layers.
words.simpleDefinition: Default storage driver for modern Docker — utilizing a copy-on-write mechanism to manage layered filesystems efficiently. It enables rapid container startup and minimal disk overhead by stacking multiple read-only image layers into a single, unified view for the running process.
words.longDefinition: Overlay2 is the preferred storage driver for Linux distributions, providing superior performance and stability compared to older drivers like aufs or devicemapper. It works by creating a 'merged' view of several directories, where the 'lower' directories are the read-only image layers and the 'upper' directory is the container's writable layer. This allows Docker to share common files across many containers on the same host while maintaining the isolation required for each individual instance.
words.connector: Think of Overlay2 like a stack of transparent sheets — you can see everything below, but you only write on the top sheet.
words.techContext: We had to upgrade our kernel to ensure we could use the overlay2 driver for better disk I/O performance.
words.exampleSentence: Docker uses "Overlay2" as its default copy-on-write storage driver on most Linux systems.

codeExample.code:
# Check the storage driver currently in use by Docker
docker info | grep "Storage Driver"
